summary: Stancil argues that analytics is often seen as undervalued not just because of mislabeling or bad tooling, but because the work itself is genuinely hard and average results can be underwhelming. The field’s value depends on exceptional analysts, yet most teams can’t consistently produce that level of impact, so organizations may be reacting rationally. He suggests improving selection and cultivation—hiring for the right mix of skills and training the squishier parts of the job—to close the gap.
money_quotes: We analysts, I fear, are honeydews.
The average engineer, by contrast, can regularly deliver more valuable results—the consistent cantaloupe to our erratic honeydew.
But better frameworks may not be enough.
Perhaps they are seeing it, and we’re the ones who are confused.
key_insight: Analytics is hard to do well at scale, so boosting its perceived value requires better hiring and training, not just better tools or organizational sympathy.
